article_title: I replaced my local LLM with a model half its size and got better results — And it wasn't about the parameters
core_thesis: The article argues that parameter count is not the most important factor when choosing a local LLM; instead, architecture and context window size can have a greater impact on real-world performance, especially for tasks involving long prompts or extended conversations. The author demonstrates this by switching from a 20B model (gpt-oss) to a 9B model (Qwen 3.5) with a hybrid architecture (GDN) that maintains constant VRAM usage even at large context lengths, resulting in better results on modest hardware (8GB VRAM).
detailed_summary:
The author, Nolen Jonker, initially used gpt-oss 20B (a 20-billion-parameter model) on an 8GB VRAM GPU via LM Studio. While it worked for short prompts, it struggled with longer contexts due to VRAM limitations and a smaller effective context window. A colleague recommended the Qwen 3.5 9B model, which uses Gated DeltaNet (GDN) architecture—a hybrid that replaces most KV cache layers with a fixed-size memory state, keeping VRAM usage nearly constant even at high context lengths. Despite being half the size, Qwen 3.5 supports up to 262k tokens context window.

Initial attempts with Qwen failed because the author had accidentally left a 'Limit Response Length' setting capped at 1,643 tokens in LM Studio. After disabling that and adjusting parameters (presence/repetition penalties, min-p, temperature, and a system prompt to curb verbosity), Qwen generated a comprehensive UX study guide that outperformed gpt-oss. The author then performed a 'needle in a haystack' test: at 30k context, Qwen couldn't find a hidden phrase; at 60k, it succeeded, confirming the model genuinely attends to the full context. Running at 60k tokens uses 7.6GB of 8GB VRAM, leaving little headroom but demonstrating efficient memory usage. The article concludes that architecture and context window matter more than parameter count for workflows involving research, study, or long conversations.

entities.name: Nolen Jonker
entities.role: Author of the article, self-hosting LLM user with 8GB VRAM GPU

entities.name: gpt-oss 20B
entities.role: Open-source 20-billion-parameter LLM previously used by the author; limited by context window and VRAM usage

entities.name: Qwen 3.5 9B
entities.role: 9-billion-parameter LLM with GDN architecture; replaced gpt-oss and provided better results due to larger context window and efficient VRAM usage

entities.name: LM Studio
entities.role: Local LLM runner used by the author; allows parameter tweaking and context window adjustment

entities.name: GDN (Gated DeltaNet)
entities.role: Hybrid architecture used in Qwen 3.5 that keeps VRAM usage constant across long contexts by replacing KV cache with fixed-size memory

key_data_points: The author's GPU has 8GB VRAM, and gpt-oss 20B officially requires 16GB but ran via GPU offloading; however, context length beyond ~30k tokens caused VRAM exhaustion and performance degradation. (🟢 Relevant to homelab/self-hosting: highlights hardware constraints when running large models locally.)

Qwen 3.5 9B uses GDN architecture, which replaces most KV cache layers with a fixed-size memory state, so VRAM usage stays nearly constant even as context length increases—unlike standard transformers where KV cache grows linearly with tokens. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming/DevOps: understanding model architecture helps in selecting efficient models for resource-constrained environments.)

The author initially failed with Qwen because LM Studio's 'Limit Response Length' was capped at 1,643 tokens; after disabling it and adjusting parameters (presence/repetition penalties up, min-p low, temperature adjusted per task), Qwen produced a comprehensive UX study guide. (🟢 Relevant to programming/self-hosting: demonstrates importance of checking runner settings before blaming the model.)

Needle-in-a-haystack test: at 30k context, Qwen could not retrieve a hidden phrase; at 60k, it succeeded, confirming the model attends to the full context window. Running at 60k tokens used 7.6GB of 8GB VRAM. (🟢 Relevant to student/research use: validates that Qwen can handle long documents and extended conversations without losing context.)

The author avoided Qwen initially because it was benchmarked for coding, but found it excellent for non-coding tasks like UX research and study guides. (🟢 Relevant to user's broad interests: encourages evaluating models based on actual use case, not just benchmarks.)
methodology_steps: 1. Identify your hardware constraints: note VRAM (e.g., 8GB) and choose a model that fits. The author used LM Studio for easy GUI-based management.
2. Select a model with an architecture suited to your workload. For long-context tasks, prefer models with efficient attention mechanisms (e.g., GDN in Qwen 3.5) over standard transformers.
3. Download the model (e.g., Qwen 3.5 9B q4_k_m) and load it in LM Studio. Ensure GPU offloading is enabled if VRAM is limited.
4. Check and adjust runner settings: disable 'Limit Response Length' if enabled; set context window to desired size (e.g., 30k–60k).
5. Tune generation parameters: increase presence and repetition penalties to reduce verbosity; keep min-p low; set temperature low for precision tasks, higher for creative ones.
6. Add a system prompt to control behavior—e.g., instruct the model to be concise and skip preamble.
7. Test with a realistic long prompt (e.g., a study guide request). If output is cut off, verify context length and response limit settings.
8. Perform a needle-in-a-haystack test to validate context utilization: embed a unique phrase in a large text and ask the model to retrieve it. Increase context length until retrieval succeeds.
9. Monitor VRAM usage during long sessions; if near capacity, reduce context length or consider a smaller model.
conclusions: Parameter count is not the sole determinant of LLM performance; architecture and context window size can matter more for tasks involving long prompts or extended conversations. The author's switch from 20B to 9B yielded better results due to Qwen's GDN architecture and larger effective context.

Runner settings (e.g., response length limits) can silently cripple model output; always verify configuration before attributing failure to the model itself. The author's initial failure with Qwen was due to a forgotten setting, not the model.

Qwen 3.5 9B, despite being benchmarked for coding, performs excellently for non-coding tasks like UX research and study guides when properly configured. This highlights the importance of testing models beyond their advertised strengths.

For users with modest hardware (8GB VRAM), models with efficient context handling (like Qwen 3.5) enable long-context workflows that were previously impossible, making local LLMs viable for research and study without expensive upgrades.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in self-hosting, homelabbing, and productivity. As a programmer and cybersecurity enthusiast, you can apply the same principles to select efficient LLMs for code analysis, documentation, or red teaming tasks that require long context windows (e.g., analyzing large codebases or logs). The detailed methodology for tweaking LM Studio settings and performing needle-in-a-haystack tests provides actionable steps you can replicate. Additionally, the insight that architecture matters more than parameter count helps you make informed decisions when building or upgrading your homelab AI setup. The article also reinforces the value of checking configuration before troubleshooting—a lesson applicable to any technical project.
extra_advice: Consider using Qwen 3.5 9B (or similar efficient models) in your homelab for tasks like parsing long penetration testing reports, maintaining conversation history during red team exercises, or as a backend for a local AI assistant that handles extended research sessions. You could also experiment with the needle-in-a-haystack test to benchmark other models you self-host, ensuring they meet your context retention needs. If you're building automated pipelines, integrate LM Studio's API to programmatically adjust context length based on task complexity.
